variables.content:
In a Galilean-invariant two-dimensional Fermi liquid with bare effective mass $m^{(0)}$, the interaction-renormalized quasiparticle effective mass $m$ is related to the spin-symmetric dipole Landau parameter $F_{1}$ by the Landau relation $m=(1+F_{1})m^{(0)}$, where $F_{1}$ is the $n=1$ angular-harmonic Landau parameter entering the expansion $f_{s}(\overline{\mathbf{p}\mathbf{p}^{\prime}})=D^{-1}\sum_{n}F_{n}e^{\mathrm{i}n(\theta-\theta^{\prime})}$ and $D$ is the two-dimensional density of states; this relation holds under the standard assumptions of Landau Fermi-liquid theory and Galilean invariance.
      [33]

In applying the Landau-level relation to the emitter two-dimensional electron gas and extracting parameters such as the leverage factor f and the Fermi energy, the conduction-band effective mass m* entering the expression ħ e B / m* is taken to be the bulk-GaAs conduction-band effective mass (or a single value close to it), which implies that corrections to m* arising from confinement in the accumulation layer, strain, band nonparabolicity, or many-body renormalisation are neglected for the purpose of fitting the observed Landau-fan data.

For a quasi-two-dimensional interacting Fermi liquid, define the thermodynamic effective mass at the Fermi energy as $m^{*}$ (entering the density of states and specific heat), the cyclotron mass measured in a center-of-mass cyclotron-resonance experiment as $m_{c}$, and the $\ell=1$ symmetric Landau parameter as $F_{1}^{s}$; the Fermi-liquid relation
      $\dfrac{m^{*}}{m_{c}}=1+\dfrac{F_{1}^{s}}{3}$
      is invoked together with the additional assumption that the Landau parameter $F_{1}^{s}$ scales approximately with the thermodynamic density of states so that $F_{1}^{s}\propto m^{*}$ for a two-dimensional system, and under these assumptions observed enhancements of $m^{*}$ relative to $m_{c}$ for the $\beta$ and $\gamma$ sheets are attributed primarily to electron–electron (Fermi-liquid) interaction effects; this chain of interpretation presumes approximate applicability of single-band FL formulas to the multi-band lattice system and neglects some band-mixing or lattice-correction effects.
      [10]

In the Landau Fermi-liquid framework, for a fermionic system with quasiparticle effective mass $M^{*}$, bare particle mass $M$, Fermi momentum $p_F$, and the $p$-wave ($\ell=1$) component of the Landau interaction amplitude evaluated at the Fermi surface $F^{1}(p_F,p_F)$, the effective mass is related to these quantities by the Landau effective-mass relation
      $$
      \frac{M^{*}}{M}=\frac{1}{1-\tfrac{1}{3}N_{0}F^{1}(p_F,p_F)},
      $$
      where $N_{0}$ denotes the free-fermion density of states at the Fermi level (in three spatial dimensions $N_{0}=p_F^{2}/(\pi^{2}v_F)$ with $v_F$ the Fermi velocity); this relation holds under the usual Landau assumptions of well-defined quasiparticles near the Fermi surface, validity of the Landau kinetic equation, and absence of additional symmetry-breaking instabilities, and it is asserted to be applicable to the low-temperature quasiparticles of the two-dimensional helium-3 films considered here when the Landau description remains appropriate.

Assuming the standard Landau Fermi-liquid relation that the low-temperature linear specific-heat coefficient \gamma is proportional to the quasiparticle density of states at the Fermi surface (hence proportional to the quasiparticle effective mass m^{*}), the measured value \gamma=2.11\ \mathrm{K}^{-1} implies an effective-mass ratio m^{*}/m\approx 2.12 (where m is the bare fermion mass) and, under the usual formulas that relate the effective mass renormalization to the first Landau Fermi-liquid parameter, yields F_{1}\approx 3.36.

The renormalizations of the Landé factor and effective mass for the two-dimensional electron gas are parametrized by the Landau Fermi-liquid relations g/g_0 = 1/(1 + F_0^a) and m/m_b = 1 + F_1^s, where g_0 denotes the bare (bulk-silicon) Landé factor used as the noninteracting reference and m_b = 0.19 m_e denotes the conduction-band mass (with m_e the free-electron mass), and these parametrizations are assumed to be applicable for the low-density, large-r_s regime probed in the reported measurements so that algebraic inversion of these relations can be used to obtain g and m from measured A and g m.

For a homogeneous three-dimensional electron Fermi liquid with bare electron mass $M$, Landau-quasiparticle effective mass $M^{*}$, Fermi momentum $p_{\mathrm{F}}$, and noninteracting density of states at the Fermi level $N_{0}$, and with $F^{1}(p_{\mathrm{F}},p_{\mathrm{F}})$ denoting the $p$-wave ($\ell=1$) component of the Landau interaction amplitude evaluated at equal incoming and outgoing momenta $p_{\mathrm{F}}$, the quasiparticle effective mass satisfies the Landau–Fermi–liquid relation
      $$
      \frac{M^{*}}{M}=\frac{1}{1-\dfrac{N_{0}F^{1}(p_{\mathrm{F}},p_{\mathrm{F}})}{3}}.
      $$
      [8]

In a homogeneous, Galilean-invariant electron liquid where the microscopic Hamiltonian conserves total momentum and electrons have bare band mass $m_b$, define the quasiparticle effective mass $m^*$ entering the low-energy dispersion of quasiparticles near the Fermi surface. The proposition is: for the $\ell=1$ density channel Landau parameter $F_1^s$ (defined as $F_1^s=\tfrac{1}{2}(F_1^{\uparrow\uparrow}+F_1^{\uparrow\downarrow})$), the relation $\frac{m_b}{m^*}=\frac{1}{1+F_1^s/d}$ holds (with $d$ the spatial dimensionality), i.e. the usual Fermi-liquid identity connecting band mass, quasiparticle mass, and $F_1^s$ is valid.

Using the Landau relation $(m^*/m)=1+F_{1}^{s}/3$, the calculated effective-mass ratio $\tilde m=2.99$ implies the Landau parameter
      $F_{1}^{s}=3(\tilde m-1)=5.97$.

Enforcing Galilean invariance in the bosonic Fermi-surface formulation yields the standard Landau relations between effective mass $m^{*}$ and bare mass $m$ in terms of the first harmonic $F_{1}$ of the dimensionless Landau interaction $F(\theta)=N(0)f(\theta)$. Specifically, with $F_{1}=N(0)f_{1}$ (where $f_{1}$ is the $n=1$ angular harmonic of the quasiparticle interaction) one obtains
      $$
      \frac{m^{*}}{m}=1+\frac{F_{1}}{3}\quad\text{in three dimensions,}
      $$
      and
      $$
      \frac{m^{*}}{m}=1+\frac{F_{1}}{2}\quad\text{in two dimensions,}
      $$
      reproducing the familiar Landau effective-mass relations.
